gaps.description: The Bill of Rights (BoR) exists only as a scaffold with all articles empty, awaiting human authorship. The MSP explicitly subordinates itself to the BoR, and Arbiter cannot enforce any scope boundaries without it. Consequently, the DIO lacks any constitutional definition of enterprise scope, prohibited activities, or core identity, leaving all actions ungoverned at the highest level.

The MSP bootstrap seed contains no substantive rules, meaning Nomos evaluates Action Proposals against an empty rule set. The system is explicitly in a default-permissive evaluation mode, allowing any action to pass the MSP compliance gate. Although the seed was deployed to "exit Nomos default-permissive mode," the absence of rules means the gate is effectively still open.

Article V of the Bill of Rights, which should define how the BoR is amended, is empty. While the BoR states only the human principal may modify it, no process is specified for doing so. This creates a procedural deadlock: even if the human principal wishes to fill the BoR, there is no sanctioned mechanism to enact the change, preventing closure of Gap-001.

The MSP references Genome documents that should extend the MSP with organ-specific operating rules and risk-tier-specific constraints. None have been created. Organs such as SafeVault, Autoheal, and Spine are operating without these detailed constraints, leaving significant operational gaps (e.g., backup failures, auto-remediation loops) unaddressed by governance.

legacy.raw_response:
{
  "gaps": [
    {
      "id": "gap-001",
      "title": "Bill of Rights Not Yet Defined",
      "description": "The Bill of Rights (BoR) exists only as a scaffold with all articles empty, awaiting human authorship. The MSP explicitly subordinates itself to the BoR, and Arbiter cannot enforce any scope boundaries without it. Consequently, the DIO lacks any constitutional definition of enterprise scope, prohibited activities, or core identity, leaving all actions ungoverned at the highest level.",
      "missionReference": "MSP — Relationship to the Bill of Rights: \"The MSP is strictly subordinate to the Bill of Rights\" and \"Scope expansion requires a BoR amendment, which is human-only.\" Also BoR document status: \"Scaffold — awaiting human authorship.\"",
      "criticality": "critical",
      "urgency": "immediate",
      "impact": "severe",
      "priorityScore": 64,
      "reasoning": "Without a BoR, the entire governance stack (Arbiter scope gate, Nomos rule evaluation) has no foundation. The DIO operates with no human-defined boundaries, invalidating the purpose of the protocol. Every subsequent gap depends on this one."
    },
    {
      "id": "gap-002",
      "title": "MSP Rules Remain Empty (Default-Permissive State)",
      "description": "The MSP bootstrap seed contains no substantive rules, meaning Nomos evaluates Action Proposals against an empty rule set. The system is explicitly in a default-permissive evaluation mode, allowing any action to pass the MSP compliance gate. Although the seed was deployed to \"exit Nomos default-permissive mode,\" the absence of rules means the gate is effectively still open.",
      "missionReference": "MSP — Rules section: \"The bootstrap seed contains no rules. The DIO operates under default-permissive MSP evaluation until Senate publishes substantive rules.\" and Purpose: \"Nomos evaluates … An AP must pass both gates to be authorized.\"",
      "criticality": "critical",
      "urgency": "immediate",
      "impact": "severe",
      "priorityScore": 64,
      "reasoning": "The operational constitution is empty. All Action Proposals are authorized by default, bypassing the MSP's intended safety constraints. The DIO is live, performing complex activities (backups, organ state changes) without any machine-enforced limits, contradicting the MSP's purpose as a compliance gate."
    },
    {
      "id": "gap-003",
      "title": "BoR Amendment Protocol Undefined",
      "description": "Article V of the Bill of Rights, which should define how the BoR is amended, is empty. While the BoR states only the human principal may modify it, no process is specified for doing so. This creates a procedural deadlock: even if the human principal wishes to fill the BoR, there is no sanctioned mechanism to enact the change, preventing closure of Gap-001.",
      "missionReference": "Bill of Rights — Article V: \"[To be defined by human principal]\" and Authority: \"Human principal only. No automated system may amend this document.\"",
      "criticality": "high",
      "urgency": "high",
      "impact": "severe",
      "priorityScore": 64,
      "reasoning": "Defining the BoR is the first step toward constitutional governance, but without an amendment protocol the document is effectively frozen. This blocks the path to establishing enterprise scope and making the MSP operational."
    },
    {
      "id": "gap-004",
      "title": "Genome Documents Missing",
      "description": "The MSP references Genome documents that should extend the MSP with organ-specific operating rules and risk-tier-specific constraints. None have been created. Organs such as SafeVault, Autoheal, and Spine are operating without these detailed constraints, leaving significant operational gaps (e.g., backup failures, auto-remediation loops) unaddressed by governance.",
      "missionReference": "MSP — Genome References: \"Genome documents live under genome/ in this directory. They extend the MSP with organ-specific operating rules and risk-tier-specific constraints.\"",
      "criticality": "medium",
      "urgency": "medium",
      "impact": "moderate",
      "priorityScore": 8,
      "reasoning": "While less critical than the missing constitutional layer, organ-specific rules are essential for translating high-level policies into concrete technical limits. Instabilities observed in the world state (backup errors, organ state oscillations) indicate a need for such rules, but they are only meaningful once a BoR and basic MSP rules exist."
    }
  ]
}

{
  "gaps": [
    {
      "gap_id": "GAP-BOR-001",
      "description": "Bill of Rights Article I (Scope of Enterprise) is completely undefined. No relational perimeter exists to determine which peer interactions, information exchanges, or assistive behaviors fall within the DIO's legitimate enterprise bounds. Person-attention coverage derived from Minder observations has no constitutional limit to evaluate against.",
      "grounding": "BoR Article I — Scope of Enterprise (placeholder)",

      "description": "Bill of Rights Article II (Prohibited Activities) is completely undefined. The DIO has no certified list of relational actions that are always forbidden (e.g., coercive persuasion, unauthorized manipulation of peer state, unsolicited modifications to human principal's environment). The current Minder observations noting Leon's opposition to unsolicited modifications are operationally unenforceable.",

The relational identity of the DIO (how it presents itself to human and persona peers, its self-model) has no constitutional anchoring. Persona‐to‐human observations in Minder cannot anchor to any defined identity constraints.",

There is no specification of which decisions are reserved exclusively for the human principal, leaving open whether Leon’s observed preferences (e.g., approval before infrastructure changes) carry constitutional weight or are merely advisory. This jeopardizes all person-attention patterns that presume human override.",

{
  "gaps": [
    {
      "id": "GAP-MSP-001",
      "title": "Undefined organ availability targets and SLA baselines",
      "description": "The MSP version 1.0.0-seed contains no rules for organ availability, recovery time objectives, or service-level agreements. The observed spine-transition log shows all 26 organs repeatedly cycling between ALIVE and DEGRADED in synchronized waves, with recovery in under 30 seconds. Without SLA baselines, there is no operational metric to trigger alerts, enforce remediation, or measure degradation against acceptable thresholds.",
      "grounding": {
        "document": "MSP",
        "section": "Rules (none present)",
        "reason": "The MSP explicitly states 'The bootstrap seed contains no rules.' The purpose of the MSP is to translate the Bill of Rights into machine-actionable constraints, including operational objectives. The absence of any availability rule means there is no operational standard to evaluate the observed spine instability against; the system is therefore operating without defined safety net for organ health."
      },
      "spine_evidence": "Multiple sequences of 'missed_pong' events affecting all organs simultaneously within a 1-second window, followed by 'pong_recovered' after ~30 seconds (e.g., 2026-05-03T17:58:11–17:58:41, 17:59:11–17:59:15). This wave repeats continuously.",
      "criticality": "high",
      "urgency": "high",
      "impact": "All organs can become DEGRADED concurrently, risking total DIO outage. No automatic escalation or human-notification path exists because no SLA threshold is defined. Operational responsiveness to cascading failures is absent.",
      "priority": 9
    },
    {
      "id": "GAP-MSP-002",
      "title": "Missing heartbeat timeout and flapping control rules",
      "description": "The MSP has no rules governing heartbeat ping/pong thresholds, maximum allowed consecutive missed pongs, or flapping detection. The spine data shows large-scale, high-frequency flapping where every organ transitions to DEGRADED simultaneously upon a single missed pong window, then immediately recovers. This indicates that the heartbeat sensitivity is uncalibrated and may be misdiagnosing transient conditions as failure.",
      "grounding": {
        "document": "MSP",
        "section": "Rules (none present) — genome references and constraints",
        "reason": "The MSP is the operational constitution for Nomos evaluation; it should define invariants such as 'organs must not enter DEGRADED state more than N times per hour' or 'global DEGRADED count must stay below M%'. The absence of such invariants means the system cannot distinguish between legitimate transient issues and toxic flapping, leading to unnecessary state transitions and potential downstream impact."
      },
      "spine_evidence": "Within a ~30-minute window, every organ flips from ALIVE to DEGRADED at nearly identical timestamps (missed_pong) and back to ALIVE after exactly one pong cycle. For example, 17:58:08–17:58:11, 17:59:07–17:59:11, 18:03:07–18:03:11 all show ALL organs DEGRADED then recovering. This pattern is identical across cycles, indicating a systemic heartbeat sensitivity issue.",
      "criticality": "high",
      "urgency": "medium",
      "impact": "Excessive state churn places load on Spine, Graph, and downstream consumers. Frequent 'DEGRADED' reporting without true failure erodes trust in organ status and can mask real degradations. Could cause false-positive alerts if monitors are added later.",
      "priority": 7
    },
    {
      "id": "GAP-MSP-003",
      "title": "No runtime targets or performance baselines defined",
      "description": "The MSP contains no runtime targets for Action Proposal adjudication, organ response times, or execution SLAs. The spine log does not record actual AP processing, but the lack of baseline means the system cannot self-monitor for latency degradation or enforce real-time guarantees.",
      "grounding": {
        "document": "MSP",
        "section": "Purpose — 'translates the BoR into a precise, machine-actionable set of objectives, invariants, constraints, and safety rules'",
        "reason": "Operational objectives such as 'Action Proposal must be adjudicated within X ms' are a core MSP responsibility. Their absence means Nomos evaluates APs only against optional constraints, potentially allowing unbounded processing delays without detection."
      },
      "spine_evidence": "No direct spine evidence of AP processing latency, but the environment's current permissive mode allows untracked runtime variance. The repeated full-organ DEGRADED events could further starve processing if no deadline is enforced.",
      "criticality": "medium",
      "urgency": "medium",
      "impact": "Without runtime targets, the DIO may exhibit unacceptable latency under load, affecting user-facing actions and downstream integrations, with no automated mechanism to detect or escalate.",
      "priority": 5
    },
    {
      "id": "GAP-MSP-004",
      "title": "Absence of cadence baselines for policy amendments and rollout",
      "description": "The MSP Amendment Protocol describes a pipeline (PEM, Legislator, review, Rollout), but no operational cadence baselines exist for how frequently amendments should be processed, staged, canaried, or rolled back. The spine data shows constant flapping; if a new MSP rule were needed to dampen oscillations, there is no timing contract for how quickly the Senate-Rollout pipeline must react.",
      "grounding": {
        "document": "MSP",
        "section": "Amendment Protocol — 'deterministic staging → canary → activation → rollback pipeline'",
        "reason": "The pipeline is defined procedurally but lacks SLA windows or maximum allowed dwell times in each stage. In a situation requiring urgent operational intervention (e.g., mass flapping), the absence of a cadence baseline means the system could take unbounded time to deploy a corrective rule."
      },
      "spine_evidence": "The spine transitions show a recurring instability that may require a new MSP rule (e.g., heartbeat guarding). Without a guaranteed rollout cadence, the human principal cannot rely on timely automated policy adjustments, prolonging the unstable state.",
      "criticality": "medium",
      "urgency": "medium",
      "impact": "Prolonged exposure to known instability. The DIO lacks a formal commitment for policy velocity, which could delay mitigation of the observed flapping and erode overall system resilience.",
      "priority": 4
    },
    {
      "id": "GAP-BOR-001",
      "title": "Bill of Rights scaffold – no constitutional scope or prohibition defined",
      "description": "The Bill of Rights (BoR) is a scaffold with all articles (I–VI) marked 'To be defined by human principal.' This means the enterprise scope, prohibited activities, core identity, human authority reservation, amendment protocol, and interpretation rules are all absent. As the BoR is the supreme governing document that Arbiter uses to set the scope perimeter, its emptiness means there is no operational boundary on what the DIO may attempt to do.",
      "grounding": {
        "document": "BoR",
        "section": "Articles I–VI (all undefined)",
        "reason": "Arbiter (#190) verifies the BoR at every scope query. Without substantive articles, the scope gate effectively permits any action that passes nominal hash verification. The MSP states it is subordinate to the BoR, so no operational constraint can compensate for a missing BoR. This is a foundational operational gap because the system's very purpose and safe bounds are not declared."
      },
      "spine_evidence": "Although the spine log shows only organ health transitions, the absence of scope means if any organ (e.g., Lobe, Syntra) initiates an Action Proposal that would otherwise violate an intended scope, there is no BoR rule to block it. The current permissive mode could allow actions outside the human's intent.",
      "criticality": "critical",
      "urgency": "critical",
      "impact": "The DIO operates without a constitutionally-defined safety perimeter. Any action, including those that could cause external harm or violate privacy, security, or compliance boundaries, could be authorized by default. This is the highest-priority gap because it undermines the entire governance model.",
      "priority": 10
    }
  ]
}
